summary: Win25H2Clean manual VM pass confirmed Win32PrioritySeparation transitions 2 -> 38 -> 2 with real reboots and bounded WinSAT CPU/memory runs.

setting.name: Foreground Scheduling Priority / Win32PrioritySeparation
setting.casual_explanation: This setting changes how Windows gives extra CPU time to the foreground application compared with background work. It is a low-level scheduler setting, not a normal everyday tweak.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when chasing old gaming or responsiveness advice, or when undoing manual scheduler tweaks from third-party guides.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Changing the value can alter how much extra runtime the foreground app gets and how scheduler quantums behave. The feature area is real, but this research pass did not capture a modern primary Microsoft source that validates the exact raw 0x26 value used by the app.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft's Win32_OperatingSystem class maps Win32PrioritySeparation to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl and exposes related high-level properties such as ForegroundApplicationBoost, QuantumLength, and QuantumType. However, the modern WMI page only gives high-level boost values for ForegroundApplicationBoost and marks QuantumLength and QuantumType as not supported on newer systems. The current app writes the raw DWORD 38 (0x26), which this dataset cannot yet publish as a validated modern mapping because the primary sources captured in this research pass do not publish the exact bitmask semantics for current Windows releases.

allowed_values.label: Modern validated default not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a modern primary Microsoft source that publishes the exact raw default DWORD for Win32PrioritySeparation.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats 0x26 as its balanced foreground scheduling profile, but this record has not validated that exact raw value from a modern primary Microsoft source.

targets.notes: Tracked as an observed raw scheduler bitmask only.

app_current_implementation.notes: Guest-side reversible probe and a later manual VM benchmark pass on Win25H2Clean confirmed the current app write and restore cycle for Win32PrioritySeparation.

windows_defaults.label: Conservative baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where this dataset has not yet validated a modern raw Win32PrioritySeparation baseline

states.rationale: Do not publish a validated raw scheduler bitmask until a stronger Microsoft source is captured.

windows_defaults.notes: This record documents the feature area but avoids publishing an unsupported raw default value.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

states.rationale: Do not publish or apply the raw bitmask until a modern Microsoft source is captured.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the scheduler setting without overstating confidence.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a modern primary-source Microsoft-backed Win32PrioritySeparation preset.
tradeoffs: The scheduler feature area is documented honestly.
The raw bitmask stays blocked from validated publication.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Observed app write used as the current foreground scheduling profile.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting what the app currently writes for Win32PrioritySeparation.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a validated modern Microsoft mapping for the raw bitmask.
tradeoffs: The current app behavior is captured.
The exact raw scheduler semantics remain unvalidated.
recommended_profiles.notes: Blocked until a stronger Microsoft source for the raw bitmask mapping is captured.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Win32_OperatingSystem class
evidence.summary: Microsoft maps ForegroundApplicationBoost, QuantumLength, and QuantumType to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl\Win32PrioritySeparation. The page explains the foreground boost concept, but modern documentation does not publish the exact raw 0x26 bitmask used by the app.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for Win32PrioritySeparation
evidence.summary: The repo documents the raw bitmask interpretation for Win32PrioritySeparation and discusses values such as 0x18, 0x24, and 0x26, but this is repo provenance rather than a primary Microsoft publication.

evidence.title: Decompiled PriorityControl read/write path
evidence.summary: Decompiled PriorityControl logic opens HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl, reads Win32PrioritySeparation, and writes it back after applying the ForegroundApplicationBoost bitfield.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes Win32PrioritySeparation = 38 (0x26) for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reversible probe - Win32PrioritySeparation tuning
evidence.summary: Guest reversible probe changed Win32PrioritySeparation from 2 to 38 and restored 2, confirming the tuning value transitions on the PriorityControl path.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean manual benchmark pass - Win32PrioritySeparation
evidence.summary: Manual VM pass confirmed baseline 2, candidate 38, and restored 2 after real reboots. The same guest then ran bounded WinSAT CPU + WPR and WinSAT mem + WPR passes for both states. The measured wall-clock durations were baseline CPU 35.28s vs candidate CPU 26.04s, and baseline mem 25.05s vs candidate mem 25.11s.

decision.why: Win25H2Clean reversible probe and a later manual VM pass both confirmed Win32PrioritySeparation transitions 2 -> 38 -> 2 on the documented PriorityControl path. The manual pass also ran bounded WinSAT CPU and memory workloads after real reboots.

open_questions: Can a modern Microsoft primary source for the exact raw Win32PrioritySeparation bitmask semantics be captured?
Should the app surface this only as an advanced research setting instead of a published preset?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: baseline observed_value=2; candidate observed_value=38; restore observed_value=2; baseline CPU duration=35.28s; candidate CPU duration=26.04s; baseline mem duration=25.05s; candidate mem duration=25.11s
validation_proof.notes: Guest-side manual VM pass on Win25H2Clean; see H:\Temp\vm-tooling-staging\priority-control-20260324-201011\summary.json and the paired CPU/memory artifact zips. Decompiled PriorityControl logic in Docs/system/assets/lsc-cimwin32.c confirms the read/write path for Win32PrioritySeparation, and Docs/system/system.md documents the AABBCC bitmask interpretation.
